title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
library(dplyr)

Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.4.3

library(palmerpenguins)
Each solution below corresponds directly to the exercise with the same number in exercises.qmd. The task description is repeated for reference.

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Data Overview

glimpse(penguins)

Rows: 344
Columns: 8
$ species           <fct> Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adel…
$ island            <fct> Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgerse…
$ bill_length_mm    <dbl> 39.1, 39.5, 40.3, NA, 36.7, 39.3, 38.9, 39.2, 34.1, …
$ bill_depth_mm     <dbl> 18.7, 17.4, 18.0, NA, 19.3, 20.6, 17.8, 19.6, 18.1, …
$ flipper_length_mm <int> 181, 186, 195, NA, 193, 190, 181, 195, 193, 190, 186…
$ body_mass_g       <int> 3750, 3800, 3250, NA, 3450, 3650, 3625, 4675, 3475, …
$ sex               <fct> male, female, female, NA, female, male, female, male…
$ year              <int> 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007…



count(penguins, species)

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species       n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Adelie      152
2 Chinstrap    68
3 Gentoo      124



count(penguins, island)

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  island        n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Biscoe      168
2 Dream       124
3 Torgersen    52



count(penguins, species, island)

# A tibble: 5 × 3
  species   island        n
  <fct>     <fct>     <int>
1 Adelie    Biscoe       44
2 Adelie    Dream        56
3 Adelie    Torgersen    52
4 Chinstrap Dream        68
5 Gentoo    Biscoe      124



penguins |>
  summarise(
    min_body_mass = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_body_mass = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_above_5000  = sum(body_mass_g > 5000, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_missing     = sum(is.na(body_mass_g))
  )

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  min_body_mass max_body_mass n_above_5000 n_missing
          <int>         <int>        <int>     <int>
1          2700          6300           61         2

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Solution 1 → Exercise 1: filter_out()
Task: Drop rows where island == "Torgersen" and body_mass_g > 4000 are both true. Rows with NA should be kept.

penguins_small <- tibble(
  species     = rep("Adelie", 5),
  island      = c("Torgersen", "Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen", "Torgersen"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 3300, 4500, NA_real_)
)


# filter_out() drops rows where ALL listed conditions are TRUE.
# Rows where any condition is NA are kept — no extra is.na() calls needed.
penguins_small |>
  filter_out(island == "Torgersen", body_mass_g > 4000)

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Biscoe           3800
3 Adelie  Dream            3300
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA


Why filter_out() is better:

Intent is explicit: you’re dropping, not keeping
NA rows are preserved automatically
No need to negate (!) or manually guard against NAs

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Solution 2 → Exercise 2: when_any() (simple OR)
Task: Keep rows where species == "Adelie" or body_mass_g > 5000.

penguins |>
  filter(when_any(
    species == "Adelie",
    body_mass_g > 5000
  )) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 213 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3450
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        3650
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        3625
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        3475
10 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
# ℹ 203 more rows


Why when_any() is better:

Makes the OR logic explicit and readable at a glance
Each condition sits on its own line — easy to add or remove conditions
Consistent syntax with the nested when_any(when_all(...)) pattern (Solution 3)

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Solution 3 → Exercise 3: when_any() + when_all() (complex OR)
Task: Keep rows where: - Group A: species == "Adelie" AND island == "Torgersen" AND body_mass_g > 3700, or - Group B: species == "Gentoo" AND body_mass_g > 5000

penguins_filters <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie",
                  "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Torgersen", "Torgersen", "Dream", "Biscoe",
                  "Biscoe",    "Dream",     "Biscoe", "Dream"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 3250, NA_real_,
                  4500, 5700, 4450, 5700)
)


# when_all() bundles AND conditions into one unit.
# when_any() then applies OR across those bundles.
penguins_filters |>
  filter(
    when_any(
      when_all(species == "Adelie", island == "Torgersen", body_mass_g > 3700),
      when_all(species == "Gentoo", body_mass_g > 5000)
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
3 Gentoo  Dream            5700
4 Gentoo  Dream            5700


Why when_any() + when_all() is better:

Groups AND conditions together visually instead of relying on () nesting
The two groups are clearly separated — easy to add a third group
Matches how you’d describe the logic in plain English: “any of these groups”

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Solution 4 → Exercise 4: when_all() (AND)
Task: Keep rows where species == "Gentoo" and body_mass_g > 5000.

penguins_when_all <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie",
                  "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Torgersen", "Dream", "Biscoe", "Torgersen",
                  "Biscoe",    "Dream", "Biscoe", "Dream"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 3250, NA_real_,
                  4500, 5700, 4450, 5700)
)


# when_all() makes AND logic explicit.
penguins_when_all |>
  filter(when_all(species == "Gentoo", body_mass_g > 5000))

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  species island body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>        <dbl>
1 Gentoo  Dream         5700
2 Gentoo  Dream         5700


Why when_all() is better:

Symmetrical with when_any() — one consistent set of verbs for boolean logic
Easy to nest inside when_any() when your logic grows more complex
For simple AND, commas in filter() still work fine too


Rule of thumb: For simple & conditions, plain commas in filter() are fine. Reach for when_all() when you need to nest it inside when_any().

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Solution 5 → Exercise 5: recode_values() inline
Task: Add an island_location column using the mapping Biscoe → Southwest, Dream → Northwest, Torgersen → Northwest.

penguins_islands <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen", "Biscoe"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 4750, 5000)
)


# recode_values() uses formula syntax: "old_value" ~ "new_value"
# No repetitive == checks needed.
penguins_islands |>
  mutate(
    island_location = island |>
      recode_values(
        "Biscoe"    ~ "Southwest",
        "Dream"     ~ "Northwest",
        "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest"
      )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750 Southwest      
2 Adelie  Dream            3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750 Northwest      
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000 Southwest      


Why recode_values() is better:

No repeated island == on every line
Formula syntax ("old" ~ "new") clearly states input → output
Unmatched values are NA by default; use unmatched = "error" to catch surprises

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
section: Solution 6 → Exercise 6: recode_values() with a lookup table
text:
Solution 6 → Exercise 6: recode_values() with a lookup table
Task: Same island → location mapping, but sourced from the island_lookup table.

island_lookup <- tibble(
  from = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen"),
  to   = c("Southwest", "Northwest", "Northwest")
)

island_lookup

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  from      to       
  <chr>     <chr>    
1 Biscoe    Southwest
2 Dream     Northwest
3 Torgersen Northwest



# Pass the lookup table's columns directly to from = / to =
penguins_islands |>
  mutate(
    island_location = island |>
      recode_values(
        from = island_lookup$from,
        to   = island_lookup$to
      )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750 Southwest      
2 Adelie  Dream            3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750 Northwest      
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000 Southwest      


Why recode_values() with a lookup table is better:

The mapping lives in a data frame — easy to load from a CSV or database
Adding or changing a mapping means editing the table, not the pipeline
Works seamlessly with from = / to = vectors of any length

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Solution 7 → Exercise 7: replace_values()
Task: In island_location, replace "Northwest" with "Northern Palmer Archipelago". Leave other values unchanged.

penguins_locations <- tibble(
  species         = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo"),
  island          = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen", "Biscoe"),
  body_mass_g     = c(3750, 3800, 4750, 5000),
  island_location = c("Southwest", "Northwest", "Northwest", "Southwest")
)


# replace_values() replaces exact matches in place.
# Unmatched values are left as-is — no .default needed.
penguins_locations |>
  mutate(
    island_location = island_location |>
      replace_values("Northwest" ~ "Northern Palmer Archipelago")
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location            
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl> <chr>                      
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750 Southwest                  
2 Adelie  Dream            3800 Northern Palmer Archipelago
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750 Northern Palmer Archipelago
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000 Southwest                  


Why replace_values() is better:

No .default = island_location boilerplate
Intent is clear: you’re replacing a value, not rebuilding the column
Use multiple formulas to replace several values in one call

title: Solutions: dplyr 1.2.0 New Functions
text:
Solution 8 → Exercise 8: replace_when()
Task: Cap body_mass_g at 5000 g. Values above 5000 become 5000; everything else stays the same.

penguins_large <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Biscoe", "Dream",  "Biscoe", "Biscoe"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 5200, 5400)
)


# replace_when() takes: the column to modify, then condition ~ replacement pairs.
# Rows that don't match any condition keep their original value automatically.
penguins_large |>
  mutate(
    body_mass_g = replace_when(
      body_mass_g,
      body_mass_g > 5000 ~ 5000
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>        <dbl>
1 Adelie  Biscoe        3750
2 Adelie  Dream         3800
3 Gentoo  Biscoe        5000
4 Gentoo  Biscoe        5000


Why replace_when() is better:

No .default = body_mass_g boilerplate
Clear that you’re modifying an existing column, not building a new one
Use multiple condition ~ value pairs to handle several replacements at once

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
dplyr functions
So many helpful functions!


⬢ distinct()
⬢ slice()
⬢ count()
⬢ pull()
⬢ relocate()
⬢ rename()
⬢ *_join()
⬢ …

But for now, let’s focus on:
⬢ filter()
⬢ mutate() + case_when()


dplyr.tidyverse.org

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
filter() is ambiguous!
monthly_losses_data |>
  filter(island == "Torgersen")

Are you keeping (filtering in) Torgersen or dropping (filtering out) Torgersen?

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: filter() is optimized for keeping rows, but dropping rows can require complex logic
text:
filter() is optimized for keeping rows, but dropping rows can require complex logic
Example: Drop rows where island is Torgersen and body mass is greater than 4000g.







    

Using filter() with negation drops BOTH the target row AND rows with NA!

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: The problem with using filter() to exclude
text:
The problem with using filter() to exclude
To properly use filter(), we would need to do something like:

penguins_NA |>
  filter(
    !((island == "Torgersen" & !is.na(island)) &
             (body_mass_g > 4000 & !is.na(body_mass_g)))
    )

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Work smarter with filter_out()
filter_out() drops rows where ALL conditions match (keeps NAs!)







   

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: When to use filter() vs filter_out()
text:
When to use filter() vs filter_out()


Use filter() to keep rows
penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie")
✅ Positive logic
✅ What you want to keep

Use filter_out() to drop rows
penguins |>
  filter_out(species == "Adelie")
✅ Negative logic
✅ What you want to exclude


Rule of thumb: If you’re using ! in your filter(), consider filter_out() instead!

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Confusing logic statements
Keep rows where Adelie penguins from Torgersen have body mass over 3700g OR where Gentoo penguins have body mass over 5000g.

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Layering when_any() + when_all()
The real power: nest when_all() inside when_any() for complex logic!







  

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: When to use when_any() vs when_all()
text:
When to use when_any() vs when_all()


Use when_any() for OR logic
penguins |>
  filter(when_any(
    species == "Adelie",
    body_mass_g > 5000
  ))
✅ At least one condition must be true
✅ Cleaner than nested | operators

Use when_all() for AND logic
penguins |>
  filter(when_all(
    species == "Gentoo",
    body_mass_g > 5000
  ))
✅ All conditions must be true
✅ Useful when nesting inside when_any()
✅ For simple AND, just use commas!


Rule of thumb: If you have complex | or & expressions in your filter(), consider when_any() and when_all()!

text:
case_when() wasn’t meant to recode values
case_when() evaluates multiple conditions to recode island names to locations

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
text:
Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
recode_values() matches exact values and recodes them to new values

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
text:
Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
With unmatched = "error", operation stops on first unmatched value

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: Using recode_values() and a lookup table
text:
Using recode_values() and a lookup table
The lookup table provides the mapping from old values to new values

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
When to use recode_values()


Use case_when() for conditions
penguins |>
  mutate(size = case_when(
    body_mass_g > 4500 ~ "Large",
    body_mass_g < 3500 ~ "Small",
    .default = "Medium"
  ))
✅ Complex logical conditions
✅ Comparing values with >, <, >=, etc.

Use recode_values() for exact matches
penguins |>
  mutate(location = island |>
    recode_values(
      "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
      "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest"
    ))
✅ Simple == equality checks
✅ Many values to recode
✅ Can use a lookup table!


Rule of thumb: If you’re using == in case_when(), consider recode_values() instead!

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
New replace_values() function
replace_values() replaces specific values (no need for .default)

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
New replace_when() function
replace_when() replaces values based on a condition (no .default needed!)

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
When to use replace_*()


Use case_when()/recode_values() to create a new column
penguins |>
  mutate(size = case_when(
    body_mass_g > 4500 ~ "Large",
    body_mass_g < 3500 ~ "Small",
    .default = "Medium"
  ))
✅ Building a new variable from scratch
✅ Multiple outcomes based on conditions
✅ Requires .default for unmatched cases

Use replace_*() to modify an existing column

penguins |>
  mutate(body_mass_g =
    replace_when(body_mass_g,
      body_mass_g > 5000 ~ 5000
    ))
✅ Replacing specific values in a column
✅ Unmatched values stay unchanged
✅ No .default needed!


Rule of thumb: If you want to modify some values but keep the rest, use replace_*() instead of case_when()/recode_values() with .default!

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Community feedback is sooo important



We are looking for #rstats community feedback on 3 new dplyr functions!
We're aiming to expand the filter() family:

filter() to keep rows
filter_out() to drop rows
when_any() and when_all() as modifiers

Read more and leave feedback here: github.com/tidyverse/ti…[image or embed]

— Davis Vaughan (@davisvaughan.bsky.social) November 7, 2025 at 10:03 AM

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Rules of thumb
⬢ Use filter() to keep rows
⬢ Use filter_out() to drop rows (if you’re using ! in filter())
⬢ Use when_any() for OR logic (if you have complex | expressions)
⬢ Use when_all() for AND logic (useful when nesting inside when_any())
⬢ Use recode_values() for exact matches (if you’re using == in case_when())
⬢ Use replace_*() to modify some existing columns

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
text:
library(dplyr)

Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.4.3

library(palmerpenguins)
Each exercise below uses a pre-dplyr 1.2.0 approach. Your task is to rewrite it using the corresponding new function from dplyr 1.2.0. Every exercise has a matching solution in solutions.qmd (Exercise 1 → Solution 1, and so on).

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
text:
Data Overview
The exercises use the penguins dataset from the palmerpenguins package and several small samples drawn from it. Here are some quick counts to help you reason about expected results.

glimpse(penguins)

Rows: 344
Columns: 8
$ species           <fct> Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adel…
$ island            <fct> Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgerse…
$ bill_length_mm    <dbl> 39.1, 39.5, 40.3, NA, 36.7, 39.3, 38.9, 39.2, 34.1, …
$ bill_depth_mm     <dbl> 18.7, 17.4, 18.0, NA, 19.3, 20.6, 17.8, 19.6, 18.1, …
$ flipper_length_mm <int> 181, 186, 195, NA, 193, 190, 181, 195, 193, 190, 186…
$ body_mass_g       <int> 3750, 3800, 3250, NA, 3450, 3650, 3625, 4675, 3475, …
$ sex               <fct> male, female, female, NA, female, male, female, male…
$ year              <int> 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007…



count(penguins, species)

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species       n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Adelie      152
2 Chinstrap    68
3 Gentoo      124



count(penguins, island)

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  island        n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Biscoe      168
2 Dream       124
3 Torgersen    52



count(penguins, species, island)

# A tibble: 5 × 3
  species   island        n
  <fct>     <fct>     <int>
1 Adelie    Biscoe       44
2 Adelie    Dream        56
3 Adelie    Torgersen    52
4 Chinstrap Dream        68
5 Gentoo    Biscoe      124



penguins |>
  summarise(
    min_body_mass = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_body_mass = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_above_5000   = sum(body_mass_g > 5000, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_missing      = sum(is.na(body_mass_g))
  )

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  min_body_mass max_body_mass n_above_5000 n_missing
          <int>         <int>        <int>     <int>
1          2700          6300           61         2

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
section: Exercise 1: Dropping rows with complex conditions
text:
Exercise 1: Dropping rows with complex conditions
This small sample mirrors the animation from the presentation. It includes an NA in body_mass_g that makes negation-based filtering tricky.

penguins_small <- tibble(
  species     = rep("Adelie", 5),
  island      = c("Torgersen", "Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen", "Torgersen"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 3300, 4500, NA_real_)
)

penguins_small

# A tibble: 5 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Biscoe           3800
3 Adelie  Dream            3300
4 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
5 Adelie  Torgersen          NA


Task: Drop rows where both island == "Torgersen" and body_mass_g > 4000 are true. Rows where either condition is missing (NA) should be kept.
Expected rows remaining after the operation:









species
island
body_mass_g
Why kept




Adelie
Biscoe
3800
Island ≠ Torgersen


Adelie
Dream
3300
Island ≠ Torgersen


Adelie
Torgersen
NA
body_mass_g is NA — can’t confirm it’s > 4000, keep it




penguins_small |>
  filter(
    !((island == "Torgersen" & !is.na(island)) &
        (body_mass_g > 4000 & !is.na(body_mass_g)))
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Biscoe           3800
3 Adelie  Dream            3300
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA



Your turn: Rewrite using filter_out() — see Solution 1.

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
text:
Exercise 2: Simple OR filtering
Task: From the full penguins dataset, keep rows where species == "Adelie" or body_mass_g > 5000.

# Helpful counts — how many rows do we expect?
cat("Adelie rows:", sum(penguins$species == "Adelie", na.rm = TRUE), "\n")

Adelie rows: 152 

cat("body_mass_g > 5000 (non-Adelie):",
    sum(penguins$body_mass_g > 5000 & penguins$species != "Adelie", na.rm = TRUE), "\n")

body_mass_g > 5000 (non-Adelie): 61 



penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie" | body_mass_g > 5000) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 213 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3450
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        3650
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        3625
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        3475
10 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
# ℹ 203 more rows



Your turn: Rewrite using filter() + when_any() — see Solution 2.

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
section: Exercise 3: Complex OR filtering with grouped conditions
text:
Exercise 3: Complex OR filtering with grouped conditions
This sample matches the presentation animation. Each group of conditions must all be true together before the OR is applied.

penguins_filters <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie",
                  "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Torgersen", "Torgersen", "Dream", "Biscoe",
                  "Biscoe",    "Dream",     "Biscoe", "Dream"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 3250, NA_real_,
                  4500, 5700, 4450, 5700)
)

penguins_filters

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
3 Adelie  Dream            3250
4 Adelie  Biscoe             NA
5 Gentoo  Biscoe           4500
6 Gentoo  Dream            5700
7 Gentoo  Biscoe           4450
8 Gentoo  Dream            5700


Task: Keep rows where: - Group A: species == "Adelie" AND island == "Torgersen" AND body_mass_g > 3700, or - Group B: species == "Gentoo" AND body_mass_g > 5000
Expected result (4 rows):



species
island
body_mass_g
Matched group




Adelie
Torgersen
3750
A


Adelie
Torgersen
3800
A


Gentoo
Dream
5700
B


Gentoo
Dream
5700
B




penguins_filters |>
  filter(
    (species == "Adelie" & island == "Torgersen" & body_mass_g > 3700) |
    (species == "Gentoo" & body_mass_g > 5000)
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
3 Gentoo  Dream            5700
4 Gentoo  Dream            5700



Your turn: Rewrite using when_any() nested inside when_all() — see Solution 3.

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
text:
Exercise 4: AND filtering
This sample mirrors the when_all() animation.

penguins_when_all <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie",
                  "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Torgersen", "Dream", "Biscoe", "Torgersen",
                  "Biscoe",    "Dream", "Biscoe", "Dream"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 3250, NA_real_,
                  4500, 5700, 4450, 5700)
)

penguins_when_all

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Dream            3800
3 Adelie  Biscoe           3250
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA
5 Gentoo  Biscoe           4500
6 Gentoo  Dream            5700
7 Gentoo  Biscoe           4450
8 Gentoo  Dream            5700


Task: Keep rows where species == "Gentoo" and body_mass_g > 5000.
Expected result (2 rows):



species
island
body_mass_g




Gentoo
Dream
5700


Gentoo
Dream
5700




penguins_when_all |>
  filter(species == "Gentoo" & body_mass_g > 5000)

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  species island body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>        <dbl>
1 Gentoo  Dream         5700
2 Gentoo  Dream         5700



Your turn: Rewrite using when_all() — see Solution 4.

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
section: Exercise 5: Recode island names to locations
text:
Exercise 5: Recode island names to locations
This 4-row sample matches the island-recoding animation.

penguins_islands <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen", "Biscoe"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 4750, 5000)
)

penguins_islands

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750
2 Adelie  Dream            3800
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000


Mapping:



island
location




Biscoe
Southwest


Dream
Northwest


Torgersen
Northwest



Task: Add an island_location column using this mapping.

penguins_islands |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe"    ~ "Southwest",
      island == "Dream"     ~ "Northwest",
      island == "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest",
      .default = island
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750 Southwest      
2 Adelie  Dream            3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750 Northwest      
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000 Southwest      



Your turn: Rewrite using recode_values() inside mutate() — see Solution 5.

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
section: Exercise 6: Recode using a lookup table
text:
Exercise 6: Recode using a lookup table
Same task as Exercise 5, but this time the mapping lives in a separate lookup table instead of being written inline.

# Same source data as Exercise 5
penguins_islands

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750
2 Adelie  Dream            3800
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000

# Lookup table
island_lookup <- tibble(
  from = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen"),
  to   = c("Southwest", "Northwest", "Northwest")
)

island_lookup

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  from      to       
  <chr>     <chr>    
1 Biscoe    Southwest
2 Dream     Northwest
3 Torgersen Northwest


Task: Add island_location using island_lookup as the mapping source.

# Manual: pull each pair out of the lookup table one-by-one
penguins_islands |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island == island_lookup$from[island_lookup$from == "Biscoe"]    ~
        island_lookup$to[island_lookup$from == "Biscoe"],
      island == island_lookup$from[island_lookup$from == "Dream"]     ~
        island_lookup$to[island_lookup$from == "Dream"],
      island == island_lookup$from[island_lookup$from == "Torgersen"] ~
        island_lookup$to[island_lookup$from == "Torgersen"],
      .default = island
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750 Southwest      
2 Adelie  Dream            3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750 Northwest      
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000 Southwest      



Your turn: Rewrite using recode_values() with from = and to = — see Solution 6.

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
section: Exercise 7: Replace specific values in an existing column
text:
Exercise 7: Replace specific values in an existing column
Starting from a dataset that already has an island_location column, replace one of the location values with a longer name. This sample mirrors the replace_values() animation.

penguins_locations <- tibble(
  species        = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo"),
  island         = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen", "Biscoe"),
  body_mass_g    = c(3750, 3800, 4750, 5000),
  island_location = c("Southwest", "Northwest", "Northwest", "Southwest")
)

penguins_locations

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750 Southwest      
2 Adelie  Dream            3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750 Northwest      
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000 Southwest      


Task: In island_location, replace "Northwest" with "Northern Palmer Archipelago". Leave "Southwest" unchanged.
Expected result:



island_location




Southwest


Northern Palmer Archipelago


Northern Palmer Archipelago


Southwest




penguins_locations |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island_location == "Northwest" ~ "Northern Palmer Archipelago",
      .default = island_location   # boilerplate — must name the same column
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location            
  <chr>   <chr>           <dbl> <chr>                      
1 Adelie  Biscoe           3750 Southwest                  
2 Adelie  Dream            3800 Northern Palmer Archipelago
3 Adelie  Torgersen        4750 Northern Palmer Archipelago
4 Gentoo  Biscoe           5000 Southwest                  



Your turn: Rewrite using replace_values() — see Solution 7.

title: Exercises: Pre-dplyr 1.2.0 Patterns
section: Exercise 8: Cap a numeric column at a threshold
text:
Exercise 8: Cap a numeric column at a threshold
This sample mirrors the replace_when() animation.

penguins_large <- tibble(
  species     = c("Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo", "Gentoo"),
  island      = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Biscoe", "Biscoe"),
  body_mass_g = c(3750, 3800, 5200, 5400)
)

penguins_large

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>        <dbl>
1 Adelie  Biscoe        3750
2 Adelie  Dream         3800
3 Gentoo  Biscoe        5200
4 Gentoo  Biscoe        5400


Task: Cap body_mass_g at 5000 g — any value over 5000 should become 5000. Values at or below 5000 stay unchanged.

penguins_large |>
  mutate(
    body_mass_g = case_when(
      body_mass_g > 5000 ~ 5000,
      .default = body_mass_g   # boilerplate — must name the same column
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island body_mass_g
  <chr>   <chr>        <dbl>
1 Adelie  Biscoe        3750
2 Adelie  Dream         3800
3 Gentoo  Biscoe        5000
4 Gentoo  Biscoe        5000



Your turn: Rewrite using replace_when() — see Solution 8.
